query: What total endogenic power does the south polar terrain of Enceladus emit?

row.paper: Howett, C.J.A., et al., 2011. High heat flow from Enceladus' south polar region measured using 10-600 cm-1 Cassini/CIRS data. JGR Planets, 116(E3). DOI:10.1029/2010JE003718

{'status': 'success', 'bundle_path': 'lab/urn-nasa-pds-asurgcps_slides_jovian_system', 'collections': [{'path': 'lab/urn-nasa-pds-asurgcps_slides_jovian_system', 'dataset_id': 'urn:nasa:pds:asurgcps_slides_jovian_system:document_callisto', 'title': 'Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Callisto', 'file_type': 'collection_xml', 'fields': {'Identification_Area': {'logical_identifier': 'urn:nasa:pds:asurgcps_slides_jovian_system:document_callisto', 'version_id': '1.0', 'title': 'Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Callisto', 'information_model_version': '1.18.0.0', 'product_class': 'Product_Collection', 'Citation_Information': {'author_list': 'Williams, David A.; Nelson, David M.', 'editor_list': 'Ward, Jennifer G.', 'publication_year': '2022', 'keyword': ['Callisto', 'digitized slide', 'RGCPS ASU', 'Dr. Ronald Greeley'], 'description': 'This collection, included in document_callisto, consists of 129 digitized 35mm slides primarily of Callisto, Galilean Satellite
              of Jupiter, and other related bodies. Slides are divided into two databases, diagrams and photos, but are inventoried as a
              single collection. Information about each photograph, including location, description, and mission, are in the document_database
              in callisto_diagram_database.pdf and callisto_photo_database.pdf. The photographs and documentation are from the Dr. Ronald Greeley
              archive at the Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies, Tempe, Arizona.'}, 'Modification_History': {'Modification_Detail': {'modification_date': '2022-09-01', 'version_id': '1.0', 'description': 'Initial PDS4 version'}}}}}, {'path': 'lab/urn-nasa-pds-asurgcps_slides_jovian_system', 'dataset_id': 'urn:nasa:pds:asurgcps_slides_jovian_system:document_database', 'title': 'Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System', 'file_type': 'collection_xml', 'fields': {'Identification_Area': {'logical_identifier': 'urn:nasa:pds:asurgcps_slides_jovian_system:document_database', 'version_id': '1.0', 'title': 'Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System', 'information_model_version': '1.18.0.0', 'product_class': 'Product_Collection', 'Citation_Information': {'author_list': 'Williams, David A.; Nelson, David M.', 'editor_list': 'Ward, Jennifer G.', 'publication_year': '2022', 'keyword': ['Jovian system', 'digitized slide', 'RGCPS ASU', 'Dr. Ronald Greeley'], 'description': 'This collection, document_database, consists of ten databases that describe information contained in the
              1258 digitized 35mm slides of Jupiter, the Galilean Satellites, and miscellaneous objects. Information includes the
              location, subject matter, mission, and if the digitized slide was of a photograph or a diagram used for
              presentation/education purposes. Digitized images of the photographs are in the collections document_callisto,
              document_europa, document_ganymede, document_io, and document_jovian_system_and_misc also in this bundle. The digitized slides
              are identified whether they are a diagram or a photograph and are listed in separate descriptive databases (e.g.,
              europa_diagram_database.pdf and europa_photo_database.pdf) but are inventoried in a single document (e.g.,
              collection_document_europa_inventory.csv). The slides and information about them are from the Dr. Ronald Greeley archive
              at the Arizona State University Regional Planetary Image Facility, Tempe, Arizona.'}, 'Modification_History': {'Modification_Detail': {'modification_date': '2022-09-01', 'version_id': '1.0', 'description': 'Initial PDS4 version'}}}}}, {'path': 'lab/urn-nasa-pds-asurgcps_slides_jovian_system', 'dataset_id': 'urn:nasa:pds:asurgcps_slides_jovian_system:document_europa', 'title': 'Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Europa', 'file_type': 'collection_xml', 'fields': {'Identification_Area': {'logical_identifier': 'urn:nasa:pds:asurgcps_slides_jovian_system:document_europa', 'version_id': '1.0', 'title': 'Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Europa', 'information_model_version': '1.18.0.0', 'product_class': 'Product_Collection', 'Citation_Information': {'author_list': 'Williams, David A.; Nelson, David M.', 'editor_list': 'Ward, Jennifer G.', 'publication_year': '2022', 'keyword': ['Europa', 'digitized slide', 'RGCPS ASU', 'Dr. Ronald Greeley'], 'description': 'This collection, included in document_europa, consists of 484 digitized 35mm slides primarily of Europa, Galilean Satellite

tool_input.summary: Published Cassini CIRS analyses estimate that Enceladus’ south polar terrain emits a total endogenic (thermal) power of ~15–16 GW (commonly cited value: ~15.8 GW). Stage 1 GEO-node directory walk found no Cassini/Enceladus holdings on the PDS Geosciences node, so the closest in-node data for analogous ‘integrate thermal emission to get power’ work are LRO Diviner thermal-IR calibrated/derived products (Moon). For the actual Enceladus determination, the supporting observations are Cassini CIRS thermal data (archived at the PDS Rings-Moons Systems node rather than GEO).

candidates.title: LRO Diviner Level 3 Gridded Data Product (GDR) Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: located the LRO Diviner Derived Data Bundle and its Level-3 gridded thermal products. While not Enceladus, these are the closest GEO-node holdings for computing regional integrated thermal emission (power) from thermal-infrared gridded products—methodologically analogous to how the Enceladus south polar terrain power was derived from spacecraft thermal data.

candidates.title: Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Diviner Calibrated Data Bundle
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: calibrated (RDR) Diviner thermal radiometer observations underlying derived grids; included as the in-node calibrated observation-level dataset most directly comparable to Cassini CIRS calibrated radiance data used to estimate Enceladus south polar thermal power.

parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS Geosciences (GEO) node at https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/

Directory layout and workflow:
Directory layout: mission/ → dataset_or_bundle/ → volume/ (PDS3) or sub-collections (PDS4)
This node has a mission layer. Start with pds_list_missions(node='geo') or, if you already know the mission directory from the abbreviation table, skip directly to pds_list_dataset_dirs(path='<mission>/', node='geo').
Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.


Common mission/instrument abbreviations:
  Mars Express = MEX → mex/ (instruments: HRSC, OMEGA, MARSIS, PFS, SPICAM, MaRS)
  Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter = MRO → mro/ (instruments: HiRISE, CTX, CRISM, SHARAD, MCS)
  Mars Science Laboratory / Curiosity = MSL → msl/ (instruments: ChemCam, APXS, CheMin, SAM, Mastcam, MAHLI, DAN)
  Mars 2020 / Perseverance = M2020 → m2020/ (instruments: PIXL, SHERLOC, Mastcam-Z, SuperCam, RIMFAX)
  Mars Exploration Rovers (Spirit=MER2, Opportunity=MER1) → mer/ (instruments: Pancam, Mini-TES/MTES, APXS, MB, MI)
  Mars Global Surveyor = MGS → mgs/ (instruments: MOC, MOLA, TES, MAG)
  Mars Odyssey = ODY → ody/ (instruments: THEMIS, GRS, NS)
  Phoenix = PHX → phx/ (instruments: TEGA, MECA, SSI, OM, RAC)
  Viking = VL1/VL2/VO1/VO2 → viking/ (instruments: camera, IRTM, MAWD)
  MESSENGER = MESS → messenger/ (instruments: MDIS, GRNS, XRS, MLA, MASCS)
  Magellan = MGN → mgn/ (instruments: SAR, altimetry, radiometry, emissivity)
  LRO → lro/ (instruments: LOLA, Diviner, LROC, Mini-RF, LAMP)
  GRAIL → grail/ (instruments: LGRS)
  NEAR → near/ (instruments: NLR, MSI, XGRS, MAG)
  InSight → insight/ (instruments: SEIS, HP3, RISE, IDA)


PDS3 vs PDS4:
  - PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style hyphenated identifiers (e.g. mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1). Leaf marker: voldesc.cat or voldesc.sfd.
  - PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`. Leaf marker: bundle*.xml or bundle*.lblx. Collections live in subdirectories.
  - Hybrid: some directories have BOTH PDS3 and PDS4 labels.

YOUR TOOLS (5 tools — always pass node='geo'):
  1. pds_list_missions(node='geo') — returns mission directories with descriptions. No HTTP.
  2. pds_list_dataset_dirs(path, node='geo', filter=...) — lists sub-directory names under a path. Cheap HTTP, no label parsing.
  3. pds_probe_datasets(paths, node='geo') — probes specific directories for PDS labels. Accepts a list of paths (max 20).
  4. pds_inspect_collections(path, node='geo') — scans PDS4 bundle subdirs for collection labels.
  5. pds_resolve_volume(volume_set_path, node='geo', dataset_id_hint=..., sample=8) — when a parent directory contains many
     numbered sibling volumes (RMS COISS_2xxx, COUVIS_0xxx, ATM
     MROM_*, jnomwr_*, cocirs_*, IMG mrox_*, MSGRMDS_*), this probes
     hint-ranked children in one call and returns per-child
     dataset_ids plus a `best_match` field. Use it instead of
     probing volumes one at a time when you know the target id or
     a discriminating substring of it.

WORKFLOW:
Step 1: If you know the mission directory from the abbreviation table, skip directly to list_dataset_dirs(path='<mission>/', node='geo').
        Otherwise call pds_list_missions(node='geo') first.
Step 2: Call pds_list_dataset_dirs for the relevant mission directory. Scan names and pds_hints to identify promising datasets.
Step 3: Call pds_probe_datasets with the most relevant paths (batch up to 20).
Step 4: If PDS4 bundles are found, call pds_inspect_collections on top 2-3.
Step 5: Return candidates.
Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires at least one candidate. Never dismiss a query as 'not a dataset request' — every paper-derived query has at least one
     supporting PDS dataset somewhere in this node's archive, and your job
     is to surface the closest match (with `reasoning` explaining the link).
  2. Prefer CALIBRATED/REDUCED data over raw/EDR for science queries.
     When a probed voldesc exposes `dataset_ids` with multiple entries,
     match the question's data need to the PRODUCT-TYPE token inside each
     id (typically the third or fourth dash-separated token). Common ones:
         EDR / RAW     = raw observations
         CDR / CALIB   = generic calibrated data
         RDR / TRDR / MRDR / MTRDR / BDR / SCVDR = reduced / derived imagery or spectra
         DDR / LDR     = derived / digital terrain / level-5 products
         SSB           = solar / stellar occultation (Cassini UVIS)
         SPEC / CUBE / WAV = spectra / spectral cubes / waveforms
         QUBE          = VIMS spectral data cube (the VIMS-flavoured "EDR")
     If the query mentions 'occultation' return the SSB id; 'spectra' →
     SPEC; 'calibrated radiance' → CALIB/CDR; 'mosaic / map / derived' →
     DDR/MRDR/MTRDR. When two product types both fit the question (e.g.
     SPEC + SSB for an occultation spectra query), return both candidates.
     Do NOT just return the scalar `dataset_id` and drop the rest — that
     field is only the first id in the voldesc and is often the wrong
     product type for a given query.
  3. For PDS4, use inspect_collections ONLY for top 2-3 bundles. Return COLLECTION-level LID when :data or :calibrated exists.
  4. When the SAME data is available as both PDS3 and PDS4, return BOTH identifiers — emit one candidate per identifier. Most published papers cite the PDS3 dataset_id (e.g. MESS-E_V_H_SW-MAG-3-CDR-CALIBRATED-V1.0, LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0); the PDS4 collection LID is the modern equivalent. Do NOT silently drop the PDS3 form.
  5. Stay under 20 tool calls per query. Soft cap: 15. Hard cap: 20.
  6. Only return paths you've actually probed — never guess.
  7. ANTI-THRASHING. Never re-issue a tool call with the same name and the
     same key arguments (path, paths, filter) that you've already issued in
     this trace. The result will be identical — it wastes the budget and
     blocks progress. If you're tempted to re-probe the same set of paths,
     either move to a different tool (inspect_collections, resolve_volume)
     or commit to `final_result` with the best candidate you have so far.
  8. STOP-AT-LEVEL. The output `path` field should point at a dataset or
     bundle directory (containing voldesc.cat or bundle*.xml), NOT at a
     deeper product / DATA / EXTRAS / BROWSE / SHAPEFILE / DTM sub-folder.
     Feature-level retrieval (a specific image of a specific crater) is
     out of scope for this finder; return the dataset and explain in
     `reasoning` how it covers the feature.
  9. After 15 tool calls without converging, ALWAYS prefer emitting your
     current best candidate(s) over running another exploratory call.
     A grounded near-match with a clear reasoning note is more useful
     than a 20-call trace that times out with no `final_result`.

===========================================================
STAGE 2 — DEEPER NODE-SPECIFIC SEARCH (escalation only)
===========================================================

Everything above is STAGE 1: the 5 live-HTTP tools (pds_list_missions / pds_list_dataset_dirs / pds_probe_datasets / pds_inspect_collections / pds_resolve_volume), the per-node workflow, and the Critical Rules ALL belong to Stage 1. Stage 1 reaches bundle/collection level by walking the node's directory tree. Stage 2 below is a SECOND set of tools for refining or rescuing Stage 1 — do NOT call any Stage 2 tool unless one of the escalation triggers below fires.

WHEN TO ESCALATE TO STAGE 2 (any one is enough):
  (a) The query asks for SPECIFIC observations, granules, or product IDs — not just a dataset. Example: 'Cassini ISS images of Mimas between 2009 and 2010' needs per-observation filtering.
  (b) Stage 1 has located the right bundle/collection but you still need to narrow by GEOMETRY, TIME, or a SPATIAL filter (bbox, lat/lon, ring longitude, target body, observation type).
  (c) Stage 1 produced AMBIGUOUS candidates that a faceted query can disambiguate (e.g. multiple instruments with similar names, multiple processing levels).
  (d) Stage 1 cannot reach the node's holdings index (e.g. SBN returns HTTP 403, a directory listing is unavailable). Use the Stage 2 API as a fallback path to candidates.

STAGE 2 TOOLS (this node):
  - ode_search_products_tool(target=..., instrument=..., bbox=..., pt=..., product_type=...): faceted product search across GEO/ODE holdings. Filter by target body, instrument, bounding box, lat/lon point, or product type.
  - ode_count_products_tool(target=..., instrument=..., bbox=...): cheap count before pulling a large result set.
  - ode_list_instruments_tool(target=...): enumerate instrument codes valid for a target.
  - ode_list_feature_classes_tool(target=...) / ode_list_feature_names_tool(target=..., feature_class=...): gazetteer lookup for named surface features (craters, maria, etc.).
  - ode_get_feature_bounds_tool(target=..., feature_name=...): bounding box for a named feature; feed the bbox into ode_search_products_tool.

STAGE 2 RULES:
  1. When you already have a bundle/collection LID from Stage 1, REUSE it as a filter in the Stage 2 call. Stage 2 is refinement, not a fresh search.
  2. Spend the MINIMUM number of additional calls. If a single faceted call answers the question, stop.
  3. If Stage 2 finds nothing or errors out, FALL BACK to the Stage 1 candidates and note in `reasoning` that Stage 2 was attempted and returned no refinement.
  4. The 20-tool-call budget from Stage 1's Critical Rule 5 is the budget for BOTH stages combined. Do NOT reset the counter when entering Stage 2.
  5. Every candidate's `reasoning` field must explicitly name which stage produced it (e.g. 'Stage 1: located bundle X by directory walk' or 'Stage 2: narrowed to product Y via ODE_search_products bbox filter').
What total endogenic power does the south polar terrain of Enceladus emit?

Citation_Information.author_list: Williams, David A.; Nelson, David M.
Citation_Information.description: This bundle contains the digitized products generated from Dr. Ronald Greeley's 35mm Slide Collection, the Jovian System, housed at the RGCPS, ASU, Tempe, AZ. Included is a User's Guide for a further description of the products.

VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: This volume contains Wallops/GSFC Airborne Topographic Mapper (ATM) digital elevation data of explosion craters at the Nevada Test Site. The volume also contains detailed documentation about the instrument and data set, as well as an index table.

collections.title: Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Callisto

Identification_Area.title: Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Callisto

Citation_Information.author_list: Williams, David A.; Nelson, David M.
Citation_Information.description:
This collection, included in document_callisto, consists of 129 digitized 35mm slides primarily of Callisto, Galilean Satellite
              of Jupiter, and other related bodies. Slides are divided into two databases, diagrams and photos, but are inventoried as a
              single collection. Information about each photograph, including location, description, and mission, are in the document_database
              in callisto_diagram_database.pdf and callisto_photo_database.pdf. The photographs and documentation are from the Dr. Ronald Greeley
              archive at the Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies, Tempe, Arizona.

collections.title: Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System

Identification_Area.title: Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System

Citation_Information.author_list: Williams, David A.; Nelson, David M.
Citation_Information.description:
This collection, document_database, consists of ten databases that describe information contained in the
              1258 digitized 35mm slides of Jupiter, the Galilean Satellites, and miscellaneous objects. Information includes the
              location, subject matter, mission, and if the digitized slide was of a photograph or a diagram used for
              presentation/education purposes. Digitized images of the photographs are in the collections document_callisto,
              document_europa, document_ganymede, document_io, and document_jovian_system_and_misc also in this bundle. The digitized slides
              are identified whether they are a diagram or a photograph and are listed in separate descriptive databases (e.g.,
              europa_diagram_database.pdf and europa_photo_database.pdf) but are inventoried in a single document (e.g.,
              collection_document_europa_inventory.csv). The slides and information about them are from the Dr. Ronald Greeley archive
              at the Arizona State University Regional Planetary Image Facility, Tempe, Arizona.

collections.title: Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Europa

This collection, included in document_europa, consists of 484 digitized 35mm slides primarily of Europa, Galilean Satellite
              of Jupiter, and other related bodies. Slides are divided into two databases, diagrams and photos, but are inventoried as a
              single collection. Information about each photograph, including location, description, and mission, are in the document_database
              in europa_diagram_database.pdf and europa_photo_database.pdf. The photographs and documentation are from the Dr. Ronald Greeley
              archive at the Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies, Tempe, Arizona.

collections.title: Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Ganymede

Identification_Area.title: Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Ganymede

Citation_Information.author_list: Williams, David A.; Nelson, David M.
Citation_Information.description:
This collection, included in document_ganymede, consists of 108 digitized 35mm slides primarily of Ganymede, Galilean Satellite
              of Jupiter, and other related bodies. Slides are divided into two databases, diagrams and photos, but are inventoried as a
              single collection. Information about each photograph, including location, description, and mission, are in the document_database
              in ganymede_diagram_database.pdf and ganymede_photo_database.pdf. The photographs and documentation are from the Dr. Ronald Greeley
              archive at the Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies, Tempe, Arizona.

collections.title: Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Io

Identification_Area.title: Document collection for Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, Io

Citation_Information.author_list: Williams, David A.; Nelson, David M.
Citation_Information.description:
This collection, included in document_io, consists of 292 digitized 35mm slides primarily of Io, Galilean Satellite
              of Jupiter, and other related bodies. Slides are divided into two databases, diagrams and photos, but are inventoried as a
              single collection. Information about each photograph, including location, description, and mission, are in the document_database
              in io_diagram_database.pdf and io_photo_database.pdf. The photographs and documentation are from the Dr. Ronald Greeley
              archive at the Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies, Tempe, Arizona.

collections.title: Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, The Jovian System Bodies and Miscellaneous Objects

Identification_Area.title: Arizona State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies - The Ronald Greeley 35mm Slide Collection, The Jovian System, The Jovian System Bodies and Miscellaneous Objects

Citation_Information.author_list: Williams, David A.; Nelson, David M.
Citation_Information.description:
This collection, included in document_jovian_system_and_misc, consists of 245 digitized 35mm slides of Jovian System bodies and
              miscellaneous objects. Slides are divided into two databases, diagrams and photos, but are inventoried as a single collection. Information
              about each photograph, including location, description, and mission, are in the document_database in jovian_system_and_misc_diagram_database.pdf
              and jovian_system_and_misc_photo_database.pdf. The photographs and documentation are from the Dr. Ronald Greeley archive at the Arizona
              State University Ronald Greeley Center for Planetary Studies, Tempe, Arizona.

parts.args: {"summary":"Published Cassini CIRS analyses estimate that Enceladus’ south polar terrain emits a total endogenic (thermal) power of ~15–16 GW (commonly cited value: ~15.8 GW). Stage 1 GEO-node directory walk found no Cassini/Enceladus holdings on the PDS Geosciences node, so the closest in-node data for analogous ‘integrate thermal emission to get power’ work are LRO Diviner thermal-IR calibrated/derived products (Moon). For the actual Enceladus determination, the supporting observations are Cassini CIRS thermal data (archived at the PDS Rings-Moons Systems node rather than GEO).","candidates":[{"path":"lro/urn-nasa-pds-lro_diviner_derived1/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:lro_diviner_derived1:data_derived_gdr_level3","title":"LRO Diviner Level 3 Gridded Data Product (GDR) Collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":"lro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: located the LRO Diviner Derived Data Bundle and its Level-3 gridded thermal products. While not Enceladus, these are the closest GEO-node holdings for computing regional integrated thermal emission (power) from thermal-infrared gridded products—methodologically analogous to how the Enceladus south polar terrain power was derived from spacecraft thermal data."},{"path":"lro/lro-l-dlre-4-rdr-v1/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:lro_diviner_calibrated","title":"Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Diviner Calibrated Data Bundle","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":"lro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: calibrated (RDR) Diviner thermal radiometer observations underlying derived grids; included as the in-node calibrated observation-level dataset most directly comparable to Cassini CIRS calibrated radiance data used to estimate Enceladus south polar thermal power."}]}
